data.title: Karelian Diamond Resources Plans Equity Raise to Advance Projects in Finland, Northern Ireland
data.description:
Karelian Diamond Resources raised 185,000 pounds sterling through a placement and subscription of new shares.

The diamond exploration company placed 18 million shares and issued for subscription another 6,666,666 shares, both at 0.0075 pound apiece, according to a Friday filing. Trading of the new shares on the London bourse's AIM segment is anticipated to take place on June 26.

The proceeds will be used to launch the next phase of its diamond deposit development work at the Lahtojoki diamond deposit in Finland, to assess the impact of copper mineralization at the Cappagh copper mine site in Northern Ireland, and as additional working capital.

data.title: Situation at Iran's Bushehr nuclear plant is 'normal', Russian official says
data.description:
Russian nuclear energy chief Alexei Likhachev said on Friday that the situation at Iran's Bushehr nuclear power plant, where hundreds of Russian specialists work, was "normal" and under control.

The Israeli military said at one point on Thursday that it had struck the Russian-built Bushehr facility, but later said the comment had been made by mistake. Likhachev, head of the state nuclear energy corporation Rosatom, said on Thursday that any attack on the plant could cause a Chernobyl-style nuclear disaster.

Bushehr is Iran's only operating nuclear power plant and uses Russian fuel that Russia then takes back when it is spent to reduce proliferation risk.

Asked on Friday about the situation at Bushehr, Likhachev told reporters: "So far the situation is completely under control, everything is normal. The night passed in the usual anxious but manageable atmosphere."

He added: "We continue to work in pre-mobilization mode and very much hope that all our signals from yesterday reached the Israeli leadership."

The "pre-mobilisation" comment appeared to refer to the possibility of evacuating staff in the event of an emergency.

Russian President Vladimir Putin said this week that Russia has up to 600 staff at Bushehr, including 250 permanent workers and others on temporary assignment. He said Israel had issued a promise to Russia over their safety.

Likhachev said on Thursday that some of those on assignment had been evacuated but the core staff remained for now.

Russia, which has close ties with Iran, has warned strongly against U.S. military intervention on the side of Israel in the air war that began a week ago.

Israel has stated that it launched strikes on Iran to prevent it from obtaining a nuclear weapon. Iran, which denies such an intention, has responded with drone and missile attacks on Israeli cities.

data.title: Greece's current account deficit shrinks in April
data.description:
Greece's current account deficit shrank to 2.1 billion euros in April from 2.5 billion euros in the same month last year on the back of higher tourism revenues and falling imports, Bank of Greece showed on Friday.

Revenues from tourism, a major economic driver for the Mediterranean country, rose by an annual 17.4% to 1.1 billion as foreign arrivals increased by 6.4%, the central bank said. Imports dropped by 11.5%.

data.title: China’s Hello Kitty gifts investors a mystery box
data.description:
Labubu, the toothy-grinned plush doll, has powered a 500% rally at Hong Kong-listed Pop Mart, now worth $41 bln. Investors may be betting on the firm's push into theme parks and more. But as the iconic Japanese character shows, turning a fad into lasting value is no smooth ride.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow Karen Kwok on LinkedIn and X.

CONTEXT NEWS

"Blind box" toymaker Pop Mart International, which has seen frenzied sales worldwide for products related to its ugly-cute Labubu character, opened its first jewellery store in Shanghai on June 13.

A Beijing auction house sold a human-sized Labubu figure for 1.08 million yuan ($150,275.51) on June 10, setting a new record for the "blind box" toy as it moves from craze to collectable.

People’s Daily, the Chinese Communist Party’s official newspaper, on June 20 criticised the “blind box” phenomenon, advocating for stricter regulation. The article did not mention Pop Mart by name and focused more on children and young people who were spending heavily on unmarked packets to collect cards. Share prices of Pop Mart fell 4% on June 20.

Share prices of Pop Mart jumped 496% in the past year, trading at HK$238.6 on June 20.

data.title: Italy's Annual Construction Output Rises 5.9% in April
data.description:
Italy's calendar-adjusted construction output index climbed 5.9% year over year in April, following a revised 3.8% increase in March, statistics agency Istat said Friday.

On a monthly basis, Italy's seasonally adjusted construction output rose 2.4%, against the 0.6% decline earlier.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Energy Headlines at 4 AM ET: Cameco Stock and Other Ways to Play the Overseas Nuclear Energy Boom | Chevron ...
data.description:
Cameco Stock and Other Ways to Play the Overseas Nuclear Energy Boom

There are currently no new reactors under construction in the U.S., but American companies have plenty of international business.

----

Chevron and Exxon Are Really AI Plays. We Explain.

AI needs power-and lots of it. These formerly stodgy Big Oil companies are well positioned to offer just that.

----

Oil Prices Are Jumping. What History Says Happens When Conflicts Threaten Supply.

For the past 40 years, the biggest increases in oil prices have all been driven by demand, not supply.

----

Clean Energy Projects Hinge on Senate Megabill Showdown

The tug of war over renewable subsidies and jobs has centered on senators like North Carolina's Thom Tillis.

----

History shows what has to happen for the Israel-Iran conflict to trigger an oil spike

Deposing Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and his regime may cause a sustained spike in oil prices

----

Nvidia Goes Nuclear, Investing in Bill Gates' Start-Up

NVentures, Nvidia's venture-capital arm, was part of the latest funding round for TerraPower, a nuclear developer founded by Bill Gates.

----

How a Solar Factory in the Heartland Sees the 'Big Beautiful Bill'

The general manager of the Bila Solar unit in Indianapolis, said legislation could impede plans to expand the company's solar factory.

----

Oil Edges Up as Next Steps in Iran Conflict Eyed

Investors remained tentative as they wait for the next development in the conflict between Iran and Israel. Front-month crude oil finish 0.4% higher.

----

U.S. Oil Stocks Post 11.5 Million Barrel Drop

Meanwhile, gasoline inventories rose by 200,000 barrels, off from analyst forecasts of a 400,000-barrel increase.

----

Keyera Deal for Certain Plains NGL Assets Widens Reach, CEO Says

Keyera's deal for Plains All American Pipeline's natural gas liquids business in Canada will bolster the energy-infrastructure company's scale and widen its reach into Eastern Canada, while bringing flexibility in market access and driving growth, says Keyera CEO Dean Setoguchi.

----

AI 'Factories' Are Hungry for Power. Expect 'Gigawatt-Scale' Growth.

The increase in demand isn't expected to slow soon.

----

This oil gauge signals how nervous investors are about Trump's warning to Iran

U.S. and global benchmark oil prices have seen their share of big moves since Israel launched attacks on Iran late last week. But there's one indicator that helps put into perspective just how worried investors have become about the potential scope of the conflict.

----

Energy Stocks Have Had a Mixed Start to 2025. Natural Gas Is Hot and Oil Is Not.

The energy sector is holding its own with the S&P 500, thanks to surging natural gas stocks. Oil stocks may start to catch up though.

----

Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on Secure Waste Infrastructure, Tullow Oil and more in the latest Market Talks covering energy and utilities..

data.title: Doosan Fuel Cell Set to Get Boost From U.S.-Bound Shipments — Market Talk
data.description: Doosan Fuel Cell is set to get a revenue boost from an expected increase in U.S.-bound shipments, NH Investment & Securities analysts write in a note. The South Korean fuel-cell maker will likely manufacture fuel cells for its U.S. subsidiary, HyAxiom, starting in 2H, they say. Analysts Y.S. Jung and J.H. Gwak raise their revenue forecast for the company by 8% for 2025 and 11% for 2026. They expect Doosan to win more contracts in the U.S., given the solid demand for power-generation facilities in data centers there. The U.S. fuel-cell market grows 15% to 20% annually, they note. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

data.title: PRESSR: The Department of Health – Abu Dhabi and Abbot unite to manufacture pharmaceuticals locally in Abu Dhabi
data.description:
ABU DHABI, UAE /PRNewswire/ — During BIO International Convention in Boston USA, the Department of Health – Abu Dhabi (DoH), the regulator of the healthcare sector in Abu Dhabi, and Abbott, the global healthcare leader, entered a landmark partnership to localise manufacturing of pharmaceuticals in Abu Dhabi and advance digital health solutions. Leveraging Abu Dhabi's advanced life sciences infrastructure, this milestone reflects the emirate's proactive preparedness to reduce dependency on imports, strengthen supply chain resilience, and foster a sustainable, self-sufficient healthcare ecosystem.

H.E. Dr. Noura Al Ghaithi, Undersecretary of the Department of Health – Abu Dhabi, said: "Following the recent launch of the Health, Endurance, Longevity and Medicine (HELM) Cluster in Abu Dhabi, the emirate proved to be equipped with a holistic, integrated infrastructure that fosters local manufacturing and R&D, positioning it as a regional healthcare gateway and leader in life sciences. By integrating our strategic vision with Abbott's expertise, we aim to accelerate the development and deployment of innovative healthcare products and solutions, reducing time-to-market, improving healthcare outcomes, and ensuring that breakthrough therapies reach patients faster. This approach positions Abu Dhabi as a regional leader in fostering an agile, innovation-driven healthcare ecosystem that directly translates scientific advancements into tangible benefits for our community."

The strategic collaboration outlines a comprehensive plan focusing on four key pillars including localisation of Abbott's pharmaceutical portfolio, biosimilar development, digital transformation through electronic patient information leaflets, and a robust education and workforce development initiative.

Mazen Bachir, Regional Director for Abbott's established pharmaceuticals business in the Gulf, Emerging Markets and Levant said: "Abbott is proud to announce a strategic collaboration with the Department of Health – Abu Dhabi, aimed at advancing healthcare resilience and innovation in Abu Dhabi. Building on Abbott's long-standing commitment to the UAE's healthcare system, this partnership will focus on localising existing pharmaceutical products, and jointly exploring the development of biosimilars, supported by regulatory alignment. The collaboration also includes initiatives to digitise life science product information through electronic leaflets in alignment with the UAE's digital health strategy. Another key component of this collaboration is the development of education programs to build local capabilities. Additionally, Abbott and the Department of Health will evaluate opportunities for joint research and development to strengthen long-term healthcare sustainability in the UAE."

Led by DoH, a high-level delegation has embarked on a strategic mission to the United States from June 15 to 21, 2025. The delegation will conduct over 20 strategic meetings and visits with public and private sector leaders across the U.S., aimed at knowledge exchange, investment opportunities and the signing of new agreements that accelerate the adoption of advanced health solutions.

Representing Abu Dhabi's innovation ecosystem, the delegation includes key stakeholders such as the Abu Dhabi Investment Office, Mubadala BIO, M42, Masdar City, KEZAD, PureHealth, and Etihad Cargo, New York University Abu Dhabi (NYUAD), Khalifa University, startAD and Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence (MBZUAI).

Send us your press releases to pressrelease.zawya@lseg.com

data.title: Greece stocks take another step closer to potential rating upgrade
data.description:
GREECE STOCKS TAKE ANOTHER STEP CLOSER TO POTENTIAL RATING UPGRADE

MSCI came out with its annual market accessibility review on global equity markets and things are looking pretty bright for Greece.

The index provider confirmed that measures taken by the government to improve its economic fundamentals along with market reforms were bearing fruit, putting its equities market on track to achieve developed-market status.

MSCI said measures introduced by authorities to manage pooled assets and stimulate stock lending and short-selling activity were adopted and validated by market participants - key issues that the index provider had flagged, not just in its 2024 review, but also when it downgraded the market to 'emerging market' status in 2013.

In March, Moody's became the last among major credit rating agencies to lift Greece's debt rating to "investment grade", acknowledging the efforts taken to improve debt sustainability.

Athens equities (.ATG) have jumped over 100% since 2021 and has outperformed both Europe's STOXX 600 and the broader MSCI emerging markets stocks index CBOE:EFS so far this year.

Greece has made a steady recovery from a debt crisis that started in 2009 and almost saw it drop out of the euro before an international bailout. However, as a sign of its comeback, the government has been repaying bailout loans and debt ahead of schedule.

Its banks - which had to be bailed out during the crisis - are fully privatised again and some of them have also started paying dividends.

Other index providers including S&P Dow Jones and FTSE Russell have put the Athens stock market on a watchlist for a potential upgrade to developed market status.

However, the market classification report by MSCI due on June 24 is the one that major fund managers will be keeping a keen eye on.

(Johann M Cherian)

*****

data.title: Kroger, Accenture Earnings and Leading Indicators: What to Watch Friday — WSJ
data.description:
By WSJ Staff

U.S. stock and bond markets reopen Friday after the Juneteenth holiday. Here is what else to watch:

  • Earnings: Supermarket chain Kroger, consultancy firm Accenture, CarMax and Darden Restaurants before the bell.
  • Economic data: Philadelphia Federal Reserve manufacturing survey at 8:30 a.m. ET, followed by leading economic indicators.
  • Overseas: Meeting of the European Union's Economic and Financial Affairs Council.

data.title: How DEI Investing Has Changed Under Trump — WSJ
data.description:
By Miriam Gottfried

While companies such as Target have blamed DEI backlash for lower sales, others, such as Costco, are seeing boosts to foot traffic. So how can companies' DEI commitments affect investors with a socially conscious approach to investing?

In this bonus episode of WSJ's Take On the Week, we are joined by Rachel Robasciotti, founder of investment firm Adasina Social Capital. She discusses the importance of diversity and inclusion efforts, particularly in light of the Trump administration's crackdown. Listen to the podcast here, or watch the video above.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.description: Norwegian digital technology company Spotlio will be removed from the Euronext Growth Oslo on Sept. 19, Euronext Oslo Børs said Friday.

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0409 ET - European natural-gas prices fall more than 3% following Thursday's rally, as concerns over a potentially imminent U.S. intervention in Iran ease. Fears that escalating tensions between Tehran and Israel could disrupt LNG supplies during a key stockpiling period in Europe drove TTF prices up 8% in the previous trading session. "We believe that most of the price rally is risk premium driven, but lower Norwegian flows due to seasonal maintenance is also forcing additional spot LNG purchases which adds to the price pressure," analysts at DNB say. However, they note LNG imports remain significantly higher year-on-year and are sufficient to balance the market and refill inventories. The benchmark Dutch TTF contract is down 3.3% to 40.25 euros a megawatt-hour, though still on track for a weekly gain of 6%.(giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0355 ET - Oil prices pare gains from the previous trading session, with Brent crude down more than 2% after President Trump set a two-week deadline to decide whether the U.S. will strike Iran, easing fears of an imminent military intervention. "While the oil price is likely to maintain some geopolitical premium, Brent crude could move back below $75 per barrel, after touching $79 per barrel on Thursday, as fears of a major escalation in the conflict ease," says XTB's Kathleen Brooks. Meanwhile, European foreign ministers prepare to hold talks with Iranian officials in Geneva later on Friday to press them to de-escalate the conflict. In early trade, Brent crude is down 2.3% to $77 a barrel, while the more liquid WTI for August trades 0.4% higher at $73.83 a barrel. Both contracts are headed for weekly gains. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0347 ET - Gold futures fall on fading hopes for substantial U.S. interest rate cuts, offsetting safe-haven demand from geopolitical risk. Futures are down 1.3% at $3,364.30 a troy ounce, and are on track for a weekly loss, having retreated from a multi-month high of $3,476.30/oz set on Monday. The Federal Reserve struck a cautious tone in its meeting on Wednesday, keeping monetary policy unchanged as expected but cautioning of continued inflation risks from tariffs. Higher rates typically damp investor enthusiasm for non-interest bearing bullion. Meanwhile, President Trump said it would decide within two weeks whether to join Israel in attacking Iran, increasing pressure on the latter to negotiate. Safe-haven demand has been a strong supporter of gold in the year to date, keeping a floor under prices. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0256 ET - The dollar falls after the Trump administration signaled it would delay a decision to launch strikes against Iran. The White House said Trump would make a decision on any U.S. involvement in the Israel-Iran conflict within the next two weeks. The news eases immediate fears of U.S. military escalation, providing some relief to investors, IG analysts say in a note. That reduces safe-haven flows to the dollar. Lower oil prices on the news also dents the dollar as the U.S. is a major oil producer and it boosts the prospect of the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates. The DXY dollar index falls 0.3% to 98.658, having reached a one-week high of 99.157 Thursday. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1116 ET - Base metal prices fall, with LME three-month copper down 0.5% at $9,604.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 0.8% at $2,526.0 a ton. Greater certainty around U.S. tariffs and demand headwinds will weigh on base metal prices throughout the third quarter of the year, Citi analysts say in a note. Copper looks particularly vulnerable, Citi says. Clarity around an expected U.S. copper tariff will see U.S. imports decline for several months at least, easing tight physical supplies elsewhere and bringing an associated fall in investor long positions. This will likely push prices below $9,000 a ton, Citi adds. Overall, base metal prices will likely fall over the third quarter by up to 10% when compared to spot prices, presenting medium-term dip-buying opportunities, analysts write. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0847 ET - Volvo's key end markets of trucks and construction equipment are on the cusp of recovery and its peer-leading margins should expand further, Bank of America Securities analysts write. The bank forecasts a second-quarter industrial adjusted EBIT of 12.6 billion Swedish kronor, down 31% on year. North American truck deliveries are expected to be weak and margins face headwinds from tariff costs and krona appreciation. Solid orders in Europe should offset weakness in North America though. The bank says near-term profit expectations are now sufficiently rebased, while re-rating potential will outweigh any further cuts to earnings expectations. "The current share price therefore offers a particularly attractive entry point." BofA Securities lifts its american depositary receipt price target to $36 from $35. Volvo's ADRs closed at $26.72. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0809 GMT - European natural-gas prices fall more than 3% following Thursday's rally, as concerns over a potentially imminent U.S. intervention in Iran ease. Fears that escalating tensions between Tehran and Israel could disrupt LNG supplies during a key stockpiling period in Europe drove TTF prices up 8% in the previous trading session. "We believe that most of the price rally is risk premium driven, but lower Norwegian flows due to seasonal maintenance is also forcing additional spot LNG purchases which adds to the price pressure," analysts at DNB say. However, they note LNG imports remain significantly higher year-on-year and are sufficient to balance the market and refill inventories. The benchmark Dutch TTF contract is down 3.3% to 40.25 euros a megawatt-hour, though still on track for a weekly gain of 6%.(giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0801 GMT - Although Berkeley's strategy is ambitious, and there is a lot in its favor, it operates in a cyclical sector currently constrained by the wider economic environment, Interactive Investor Richard Hunter says. The housebuilder's shares have wilted given wider pressures despite its best efforts. They are currently down 33% from February 2020's prepandemic peak, and down 15% over the past year when compared to the 6.3% gain for the wider FTSE 100, he says. A low opening price is evidence that, for now, investors remain unconvinced by the outlook with its strategy still a distant dream, Hunter says. "As such, the market consensus of the shares is unlikely to break out of its current rating as a hold, albeit a strong one," he says. Shares are down 8.8% at 3,786 pence. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0750 GMT - Berkeley may see the top end of consensus come down toward company expectations despite results for the year being in line with guidance, Jefferies analysts say in a note. This is as cash due on forward sales was guided to moderate further, Jefferies says. Management says it is well-placed to achieve FY 2026 pretax profit guidance of 450 million pounds, which compares with guidance of "at least" that previously, with consensus at 470 million pounds, Jefferies says. For FY 2027. the housebuilder guided for pretax profit similar to FY 2026, with consensus at 476 million pounds, and Jefferies estimating 461 million pounds. Shares are down 9% at 3,778 pence, and have fallen 21% over the past 12 months. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0747 GMT - Gold futures fall on fading hopes for substantial U.S. interest rate cuts, offsetting safe-haven demand from geopolitical risk. Futures are down 1.3% at $3,364.30 a troy ounce, and are on track for a weekly loss, having retreated from a multi-month high of $3,476.30/oz set on Monday. The Federal Reserve struck a cautious tone in its meeting on Wednesday, keeping monetary policy unchanged as expected but cautioning of continued inflation risks from tariffs. Higher rates typically damp investor enthusiasm for non-interest bearing bullion. Meanwhile, President Trump said it would decide within two weeks whether to join Israel in attacking Iran, increasing pressure on the latter to negotiate. Safe-haven demand has been a strong supporter of gold in the year to date, keeping a floor under prices. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0722 GMT - Amundi's third-party distribution client segment is a key growth pillar, Citi says in a research note. The French asset manager updated its outlook for the segment, which is taking share in a growing market. It has achieved a 21% compound annual growth rate for its assets under management over the 2020-24 period, analysts write. The business has around 600 distribution contracts and is already well-diversified by geography and client type. The demand from distributors for players that offer breadth and efficient scaling benefits incumbents like Amundi. "We came away reassured by the strength and breadth of Amundi's offering, its strong market positioning, as well as its long-term growth trajectory, supported by attractive M&A optionality," Citi says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0710 GMT - There aren't any significant sector-specific risks that would justify a strong correction for Spanish banks, Renta 4 says in a research note. Though, markets at record highs call for caution, it says. "We believe corrections, if any, would be more responsive to the market environment in a context of high uncertainty (trade war, impact of tariffs, armed conflicts, escalating geopolitical tensions)," analyst Nuria Alvarez writes. The domestic profile of the lenders provides them with support in this climate, she says. The sector's earnings have proven resilient despite interest-rate cuts, supporting capital distributions and a share rally since the start of the year. The next catalyst is the publication of second-quarter results, in which some could lift their guidance. Renta 4 lowers its rating on Santander and UniCaja to hold from overweight. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0645 GMT - Signs are gathering that the U.K.'s strong start to 2025 is over, Capital Economics' Paul Dales writes in a note. Retail sales fell more sharply than expected in May, figures show Friday, with volumes dropping at their fastest rate since December 2023. That wipes out the gains in sales made in the first months of the year, which contributed to a surprisingly robust growth rate in the first quarter. Consumer spending may still outperform other sectors of the economy this year, Dales says, pointing to improvements in consumer sentiment. "But it looks like GDP in the second quarter was pretty soft," he says.(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0628 GMT - Sterling falls against the euro and pares gains versus the dollar after U.K. retail sales data came in much weaker than expected. Retail sales fell 2.7% month-on-month in May. Economists in a WSJ survey expected a 0.6% decline. It follows an upwardly revised 1.3% increase in April. "Despite two bank holidays and the warm weather, spending failed to kick start in May as consumers tightened their purse strings amid resurging inflation, April utility bills rises and poor labour market conditions," Ebury's Phil Monkhouse says in a note. The euro rises 0.1% to an intraday high of 0.8548 pounds, compared to 0.8539 before the data. Against a weaker dollar, sterling rises 0.1% to $1.3479, versus $1.3494 before the data.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0624 GMT - Societe Generale's digital bank BoursoBank is a key element of the French lender's investment case, Jefferies says in a research note. Analyst Joseph Dickerson and associate Theo Massing estimate that BoursoBank will earn the group 325 million euros in 2026, providing a mechanical 5% uplift to overall profits. They note that the digital bank is still investing heavily in customer acquisition though these costs are set to fall in the coming years. It is remarkable that its number of employees has plateaued at around 1,000 while client numbers keep growing, they note. The analysts see BoursoBank capable of achieving a return on equity of 25% by 2026, which supports the profitability improvement of SocGen's French retail unit. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0620 GMT - Taiwan's stock market may have a brighter outlook than South Korea's over the coming year if investors' appetite for artificial-intelligence technology remains firm, Capital Economics economist Shivaan Tandon writes in a note. South Korea's market this year is faring much better than Taiwan's, a key beneficiary of the AI boom and one of the top performers in the world in 2024, Tandon notes. "We expect Taiwan to be back on top before long," he says. While Taiwan's robust investment and dominance in the AI supply chain are expected to keep its growth stronger, South Korea's growth is likely to undershoot consensus amid struggling domestic demand and non-electronics exports, he adds. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0546 GMT - Lindt & Spruengli is expected to report organic sales growth for the first half of the year, with pricing in North America and Europe offsetting declining volumes, analysts at Berenberg say in a research note. All three of Lindt's key regional markets grew in the first half, Berenberg says. Pricing has been the growth driver in both North America and Europe, given that sales volumes declined in both regions compared with the year-earlier period, according to Berenberg. "North American markets have declined by a larger magnitude than their European counterparts," the analysts say. For the first half, Berenberg expects Lindt's organic sales growth at 8.5%, broadly in line with a company-compiled consensus of 8.7%. Lindt shares are up by a third since the start of 2025. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0509 GMT - The pace of artificial intelligence's technological change of is one of the biggest challenges for the business, says Peter Schwartz, Salesforce's chief futures officer. "Things that we're working on today may be irrelevant a year from now and certainly will be 18 months from now," Schwartz says, adding that businesses need to move fast and adapt quickly to survive in the AI age. While it is difficult to predict what AI will be doing a year from now, Schwartz says his job is to look two steps ahead and figure out what its developers should be looking at next. For example, he foresees AI robots that can do specific mechanical tasks like filling up inventory and shelves at a supermarket in the next few years. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0809 GMT - European natural-gas prices fall more than 3% following Thursday's rally, as concerns over a potentially imminent U.S. intervention in Iran ease. Fears that escalating tensions between Tehran and Israel could disrupt LNG supplies during a key stockpiling period in Europe drove TTF prices up 8% in the previous trading session. "We believe that most of the price rally is risk premium driven, but lower Norwegian flows due to seasonal maintenance is also forcing additional spot LNG purchases which adds to the price pressure," analysts at DNB say. However, they note LNG imports remain significantly higher year-on-year and are sufficient to balance the market and refill inventories. The benchmark Dutch TTF contract is down 3.3% to 40.25 euros a megawatt-hour, though still on track for a weekly gain of 6%.(giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0757 GMT - Cracks in Asia's economic growth could appear if oil prices rise to around $85/bbl, but the impact is likely to remain manageable, HSBC chief Asia economist Frederic Neumann writes in a note. Oil wouldget more attention from central bankers at that level, as it would contribute to inflation rather than detract from it, Neumann says. Still, the oil and gas intensity of Asian economies has fallen sharply over the past decade, and in real terms, oil is quite cheap at the moment, he adds. If oil rises much higher than $85/bbl, it would deliver a major hit, he says. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are 0.7% higher at $75.66/bbl; front-month Brent crude oil futures are 2.4% lower at $76.94/bbl. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0755 GMT - Oil prices pare gains from the previous trading session, with Brent crude down more than 2% after President Trump set a two-week deadline to decide whether the U.S. will strike Iran, easing fears of an imminent military intervention. "While the oil price is likely to maintain some geopolitical premium, Brent crude could move back below $75 per barrel, after touching $79 per barrel on Thursday, as fears of a major escalation in the conflict ease," says XTB's Kathleen Brooks. Meanwhile, European foreign ministers prepare to hold talks with Iranian officials in Geneva later on Friday to press them to de-escalate the conflict. In early trade, Brent crude is down 2.3% to $77 a barrel, while the more liquid WTI for August trades 0.4% higher at $73.83 a barrel. Both contracts are headed for weekly gains. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: European Gas Prices Slide As Fears of Rapid Middle East Escalation Ease — Market Talk
data.description:
0809 GMT - European natural-gas prices fall more than 3% following Thursday's rally, as concerns over a potentially imminent U.S. intervention in Iran ease. Fears that escalating tensions between Tehran and Israel could disrupt LNG supplies during a key stockpiling period in Europe drove TTF prices up 8% in the previous trading session. "We believe that most of the price rally is risk premium driven, but lower Norwegian flows due to seasonal maintenance is also forcing additional spot LNG purchases which adds to the price pressure," analysts at DNB say. However, they note LNG imports remain significantly higher year-on-year and are sufficient to balance the market and refill inventories. The benchmark Dutch TTF contract is down 3.3% to 40.25 euros a megawatt-hour, though still on track for a weekly gain of 6%.(giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0802 GMT - The U.K. public sector borrowing for May stood at 17.7 billion pounds ($23.8 billion), below the 18.8 billion pounds forecast by economists in a WSJ survey. It was the second highest May borrowing level on record. The lower-than-expected borrowing is unlikely to alleviate concerns about U.K.'s fiscal sustainability, XTB's Kathleen Brooks says in a note. U.K. government borrowing remains elevated, Brooks says. "This cycle is not sustainable, and bond vigilantes could be woken from their slumber by more data like this." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0755 GMT - A pause in trade-in subsidies will likely slow auto sales in the near term, Daiwa analysts write in a note. At least six Chinese cities and municipalities have suspended trade-in subsidies for car buyers in June, they add, citing a media report. While Shenyang and Chongqing said the suspension was due to adjustments to improve capital efficiency, most other cities attributed the pause to the depletion of the first round of nationwide funding totaling 162 billion yuan, they say. Car sales should accelerate after the second round of subsidies totaling 138 billion yuan is effectively allocated by the central government later this year, they say. Daiwa expects the auto sector to remain under pressure until July, with sentiment likely to recover toward the end of the third quarter.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0750 GMT - Tax increases in the U.K. later this year appear increasingly likely given U.K. public-sector borrowing figures, Capital Economics' Alex Kerr says in a note. Borrowing was 17.7 billion pounds in May, higher than fiscal watchdog OBR's forecast of 17.1 billion pounds and the second highest May borrowing level on record. The situation is unlikely to improve for the government given any buffer against its fiscal mandate may be wiped out at the Autumn budget, Kerr says. The cooling labor market will likely keep a lid on tax receipts. "With the gilt market sensitive to significant increases in borrowing, all this means tax rises are looking increasingly likely," he adds. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0747 GMT - Gold futures fall on fading hopes for substantial U.S. interest rate cuts, offsetting safe-haven demand from geopolitical risk. Futures are down 1.3% at $3,364.30 a troy ounce, and are on track for a weekly loss, having retreated from a multi-month high of $3,476.30/oz set on Monday. The Federal Reserve struck a cautious tone in its meeting on Wednesday, keeping monetary policy unchanged as expected but cautioning of continued inflation risks from tariffs. Higher rates typically damp investor enthusiasm for non-interest bearing bullion. Meanwhile, President Trump said it would decide within two weeks whether to join Israel in attacking Iran, increasing pressure on the latter to negotiate. Safe-haven demand has been a strong supporter of gold in the year to date, keeping a floor under prices. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0739 GMT - The euro rises back above the key $1.15 level as markets price out some geopolitical risks from the exchange rate but gains should remain limited, ING analyst Francesco Pesole says in a note. That follows news that President Trump will decide on whether to directly involve the U.S. in the Israel-Iran conflict within two weeks, easing concerns over immediate action. "The situation in the Middle East remains too volatile to make a strong directional call on [euro versus the dollar], but the overarching risk of the U.S. joining the conflict could keep it from aggressively retesting $1.1600 in the next few days." The euro rises 0.2% to $1.1518, having reached a one-week low of $1.1445 Thursday, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0723 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds fall following weaker-than-forecast retail sales data. The monthly retail sales contracted 2.7% in May, weaker than the consensus forecast of 0.6% contraction by economists in a WSJ poll. The data raises the possibility of the Bank of England cutting interest rates in August, Omnis Investments' Patrick O'Donnell says in a note. The 10-year gilt yields fall around 3 basis points to last trade at 4.501%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0716 GMT - Bitcoin rises as news that President Trump would delay a decision on U.S. involvement in the Israel-Iran conflict improves risk sentiment. The White House said Trump would make a decision within the next two weeks. "Prior to this, several sources had pointed to a U.S. strike on Iran possibly happening as soon as this weekend which led to a risk-off mood yesterday in the quiet U.S.-holiday influenced session," Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. Hence, the latest news should reduce the event risk ahead of the weekend, they say. Bitcoin rises 0.4% to $104,709, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0711 GMT - A slump in Britain's retail sales last month can in part be put down to the timing of Easter and the unusually good early springtime weather, Rob Wood and Elliott Jordan-Doak at Pantheon Macroeconomics write in a note. Volumes dropped by 2.7% in May, the fastest monthly decline in close to 18 months, figures from the U.K. statistics agency show Friday. But much of that volatility can be pinned to the Easter holiday, which this year fell late in April and is to blame for the 5% drop in food-store sales, "down from the Easter Egg-fuelled 4.7% jump in April," Wood and Jordan-Doak note. Early home-improvement work, as homeowners made the most of sunny weather in March and April, meanwhile helps explain the drop in sales of household goods in May, they say. "We think the underlying upward trend of consumer spending growth will resume in June," they add. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0704 GMT - Eurozone government bond spreads over Bunds generally remain at historically tight levels, but the room for further narrowing is limited near term, ING's Benjamin Schroeder says in a note. "The appetite for aggressive tightening positions at such levels will be limited going into a typically less liquid and more volatile summer period," the senior rates strategist says. In addition, uncertainties around geopolitics and trade relations with key dates are still coming up, he says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0656 GMT - The dollar falls after the Trump administration signaled it would delay a decision to launch strikes against Iran. The White House said Trump would make a decision on any U.S. involvement in the Israel-Iran conflict within the next two weeks. The news eases immediate fears of U.S. military escalation, providing some relief to investors, IG analysts say in a note. That reduces safe-haven flows to the dollar. Lower oil prices on the news also dents the dollar as the U.S. is a major oil producer and it boosts the prospect of the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates. The DXY dollar index falls 0.3% to 98.658, having reached a one-week high of 99.157 Thursday. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0653 GMT - Eurozone peripheral government bonds become more attractive in Citi's global asset allocation in June versus German Bunds due to better economic prospects. "The European periphery replaces our longstanding Bunds overweight [initiated on March 31]," they say in a note. "Improving economic trends in Europe suggest a further spread tightening of the periphery versus Bunds," they say. With Citi's model signaling long Italian BTPs, Citi decides to flip Bunds overweight to a BTP overweight. The 10-year BTP yield is trading at 3.502%, down 4 bps, while the 10-year Bund yield falls 2.4 bps to last trade at 2.492%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Almost a Third of Eurozone's U.S. Trade Surplus Is Due to U.S. Firms, Says ECB
data.description:
By Paul Hannon

Almost a third of the eurozone's goods trade surplus with the U.S. is accounted for by sales of products manufactured by the affiliates of American businesses, which also account for most of the eurozone's deficit in the trade in services, the European Central Bank said Friday.

In its latest Economic Bulletin, economists at the central bank said that should the activities carried out by those affiliates be moved back to the U.S. in response to higher tariffs or changes to U.S. tax policy, the eurozone economy would be smaller, but the impact on employment and incomes would likely be limited.

"Over a longer period, productivity may suffer if the domestic economy is no longer gaining the positive spillovers," the ECB's economists wrote.

The ECB's research highlights the complexity of trade and investment relations between the eurozone and the U.S., and the outsized role played by Ireland in the trans-Atlantic economy.

Many of the U.S. firms that contribute to the eurozone's surplus in goods are based in Ireland, and manufacture pharmaceuticals. But they also import services from the U.S. such as the use of intellectual property, while sending large profits back to their American shareowners.

The ECB said that in 2024, the flows of goods, services and profits between the U.S. and the eurozone broadly evened out, even if the eurozone had a large surplus in the trade in goods.

"The euro area current account with the United States was nearly balanced in 2024, as the increasing surplus in goods trade was almost entirely offset by deficits in services trade and foreign direct investment income," the ECB said.

The eurozone operations of U.S. multinational enterprises contributed "significantly" to those flows, the ECB said. Its economists estimated that 30% of the eurozone's surplus in goods was due to the sales of U.S. businesses, while those same companies accounted for 90% of the eurozone's services deficit.

"The widening goods trade surplus vis-à-vis the United States is driven mostly by a pronounced increase in exports of pharmaceutical products, which are mostly attributed to trade flows of Irish affiliates of U.S. MNEs," the ECB said.

While Ireland can offer skilled workers familiar with U.S. regulatory requirements to the pharmaceuticals industry, it is also attractive as a way of lowering their tax bills.

President Trump has made it clear that he is unhappy that such a large share of pharmaceutical production for U.S. consumption is located overseas.

The ECB's economists warned that higher tariffs on imports from the eurozone could lead U.S. businesses to move some of their production back home or to other locations.

However, while that would reduce the size of the eurozone economy, the ECB said it would have a much smaller impact on employment and incomes because those activities are highly capital intensive rather than job-rich, and most of the profits are sent back to the U.S.

Write to Paul Hannon at paul.hannon@wsj.com

data.title: Italy Construction Output Growth Accelerates
data.description:
Construction output in Italy increased by 5.9% year-on-year in April 2025, accelerating from a downwardly revised 3.8% rise in the previous month.

This marked the fourth consecutive month of growth and the largest gain since July 2024.

On a seasonally adjusted monthly basis, output jumped by 2.4% in April, rebounding from a 0.6% fall in March.

data.title: Reddit Insider Sold Shares Worth $4,148,287, According to a Recent SEC Filing
data.description:
Jennifer L. Wong, Chief Operating Officer, on June 16, 2025, sold 33,333 shares in Reddit for $4,148,287. Following the Form 4 filing with the SEC, Wong has control over a total of 1,589,007 Class A common shares of the company, with 1,428,007 shares held directly and 161,000 controlled indirectly.

SEC Filing:

www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1713445/000171344525000162/xslF345X05/wk-form4_1750291754.xml

data.title: Poonawalla Fincorp Gets Board Nod to Raise Up To INR16 Billion Through Debentures
data.description:
Poonawalla Fincorp has received board approval to raise up to 16 billion Indian rupees through the issue of non-convertible debentures, on a private placement basis, according to a Friday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The company plans to issue secured, redeemable, rated, listed, non-convertible debentures of face value of 100,000 rupees each in two tranches.

The debentures are proposed to be listed on the BSE.

The company's shares were up nearly 1% in recent trade.

data.title: U.K. Public Sector Finances Data Unlikely to Ease Fiscal Concerns — Market Talk
data.description: The U.K. public sector borrowing for May stood at 17.7 billion pounds ($23.8 billion), below the 18.8 billion pounds forecast by economists in a WSJ survey. It was the second highest May borrowing level on record. The lower-than-expected borrowing is unlikely to alleviate concerns about U.K.'s fiscal sustainability, XTB's Kathleen Brooks says in a note. U.K. government borrowing remains elevated, Brooks says. "This cycle is not sustainable, and bond vigilantes could be woken from their slumber by more data like this." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: Furukawa Electric Co Ltd (Update On Disclosed Matter) Notice Concerning A Change In Scheduled Commencement Of Tender Offer For Shares Of Our Subsidiary
data.description:
Furukawa Electric Co Ltd :

  • FURUKAWA ELECTRIC CO LTD: (UPDATE ON DISCLOSED MATTER) NOTICE CONCERNING A CHANGE IN SCHEDULED COMMENCEMENT OF TENDER OFFER FOR SHARES OF OUR SUBSIDIARY
